behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the correlation/grouping logic itself (trace_id keying), not real I/O. MOCK-MISS: this behavior can fail inside one local grouping function (e.g., accidental key includes service name). REAL-INFRA/LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP are not required by the behavior text. Rejected Integration because the text does not require module handoff; it only specifies grouping outcome.
behaviors.test_description: Call the correlator/grouping function with an in-memory set of parsed log objects from multiple services sharing a trace_id plus control traces. Assert one group per trace_id and that service origin does not split groups.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: Primary risk is timestamp ordering/skew-adjustment algorithm correctness. MOCK-MISS: this is still local logic; no external infra needed. REAL-INFRA does not apply because skew is represented in test data, not OS clocks. Rejected Integration because behavior text focuses on ordering rule ('up to 500ms skew'), which can be validated at the sorter/timeline function boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Feed the timeline ordering function in-memory entries from different services with controlled timestamp offsets (including ±500ms edge cases). Assert stable request timeline ordering follows skew policy and deterministic tie-breaks.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: Behavior spans at least two local modules: metric computation/detection and report emission ('flagged in the anomaly report'). A pure unit test of percentile math could miss contract mismatches where detector output is not surfaced in report. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no real OS/network/subprocess, no AI dependency, no multi-agent chain; so Integration is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Run an in-process pipeline wiring baseline calculator + p99 detector + anomaly report builder on synthetic latency data where p99 crosses 3x baseline. Assert report contains the spike flag with expected trace/window metadata.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: Failure mode is parser + pipeline control flow + reporting counter interaction. Unit-testing parser alone can miss dropped/propagated error state and incorrect skipped-count aggregation. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no required real network/OS behavior in text; malformed payloads can be injected in-process. Rejected System because behavior does not require filesystem/socket/runtime quirks, only pipeline resilience and counting.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the in-process ingest pipeline on a mixed batch of valid and malformed JSON strings. Assert processing completes (no crash), valid entries continue downstream, malformed ones are skipped, and skipped-entry metric/report count matches exact malformed input count.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: This is environment/performance behavior under burst load; real scheduling, buffering, backpressure, and runtime timing effects matter. MOCK-HIDE: in-process fakes or mocked clocks/queues can pass while real process drops messages or accumulates lag. REAL-INFRA implies System by default; no AI/agentic dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run the actual tool process with a real load generator producing 10,000 structured log entries/sec for a fixed interval. Measure ingest rate, output count, queue lag/backlog over time, and end-to-end processed count; assert zero drops and no sustained backlog growth after burst.
